Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Understand the Goal The objective is to analyze an event log in DuckDB to find the most common process variants sequences of activities per case and then return all original events associated with cases that belong to the top K most frequent variants

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyze the Input Table Name event_log Columns case_id activity timestamp Definition of Process Variant Ordered sequence of activities within a case_id sorted by timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Break Down the Steps Step 1 Construct the sequence per case Need to group by case_id Need to order activities by timestamp Need to aggregate activities into a single representation to represent the variant DuckDB Specific DuckDB has excellent support for lists Let us use LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 2 Group cases by variant and count Take the result from Step 1 case_id variant_sequence Group by variant_sequence Count the number of case_ids per sequence

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 3 Identify Top K Variants Order the grouped results by count descending Limit to K Need to keep track of which case_id values belong to these top variants so we can filter the original log later

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 4 Filter Original Log Join the original event_log with the list of case_id values identified in Step 3 Return the original columns case_id activity timestamp

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Drafting the Query Mental or Scratchpad CTE 1 Case Variants SELECT case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp as variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id CTE 2 Variant Counts and Ranking SELECT variant COUNT as freq FROM CaseVariants GROUP BY variant ORDER BY freq DESC LIMIT K

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Wait CTE 2 gives me the variants but I need the case_id values associated with them to filter the original log efficiently Better approach Join CTE 1 and CTE 2

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Window functions could also be used but CTEs are clearer for this logic Let us stick to the CTE approach for readability and logical separation as requested by the prompt structure

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Refined Plan case_variants map case_id to variant_sequence variant_counts map variant_sequence to count top_variants select top K variant_sequence values top_cases select case_id values that match top_variants final join event_log with top_cases

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Addressing Specific Constraints Correctly extract the ordered sequence Use LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp Aggregate and count GROUP BY variant Filter down to top K ORDER BY count DESC LIMIT K Return corresponding events Join back to event_log

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Finalizing the SQL WITH case_variants AS SELECT case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp AS variant_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variant_counts AS SELECT variant_sequence COUNT AS case_count FROM case_variants GROUP BY variant_sequence top_k_variants AS SELECT variant_sequence FROM variant_counts ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT 5 top_k_cases AS SELECT cv case_id FROM case_variants cv JOIN top_k_variants tkv ON cv variant_sequence equals tkv variant_sequence SELECT el all FROM event_log el JOIN top_k_cases tkc ON el case_id equals tkc case_id ORDER BY el case_id el timestamp

Name: Validation - IND
Text: Review against potential edge cases Ties If the Kth variant has ties LIMIT 5 arbitrarily cuts them The prompt asks for Top K variants so standard SQL LIMIT is sufficient NULLs Assuming standard clean event log data Performance Comparing Lists in joins can be expensive but this is the standard semantic way Parameter K I should explicitly mention that 5 is a placeholder for K

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Self Correction on LIST syntax DuckDB does support LIST column ORDER BY column inside an aggregation context Verification Yes DuckDB supports LIST value ORDER BY value inside SELECT GROUP BY Okay the syntax is valid for DuckDB

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Can I do it in fewer CTEs WITH case_sequences AS SELECT case_id LIST activity ORDER BY timestamp AS seq FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id top_sequences AS SELECT seq FROM case_sequences GROUP BY seq ORDER BY COUNT DESC LIMIT 10 Final event_log join case_sequences on case_id join top_sequences on seq This is slightly more efficient and clean

Name: Validation - PE
Text: This works and is robust case_sequences is needed to map case_id to seq top_sequences filters the top variants final select returns all events with SELECT el all Ordering by case_id and timestamp is good practice
